**Python Introduction**  
**Our First Python Program**

* ![][image1]

**Python 2 vs Python 3**

* [Python 2 vs Python 3](https://sebastianraschka.com/Articles/2014_python_2_3_key_diff.html)  
* [Python 2 vs Python 3 \- another one](https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/important-differences-between-python-2-x-and-python-3-x-with-examples/)

**Exercise: How Does Python Work?**

* Python works by sending source code to an interpreter. That reads code line by line to create a set of instructions in byte code, which a machine can understand and execute these instructions.

**ZTM Python Cheat Sheet**

* Cheat Sheet  
  * [https://zerotomastery.io/cheatsheets/python-cheat-sheet/](https://zerotomastery.io/cheatsheets/python-cheat-sheet/)  
* Course source code  
  * [https://github.com/aneagoie/All-Python-codes-of-ZTM-course-by-Andrei-Neagoie](https://github.com/aneagoie/All-Python-codes-of-ZTM-course-by-Andrei-Neagoie)

**Python Basics**  
**Python Data Types**

* Fundamental Data Types  
  * int  
  * float  
  * bool  
  * str  
  * list  
  * tuple  
  * set  
  * dict  
* Classes \- Custom Types  
* Specialized Data Types  
  * Packages and modules from libraries.  
* None \- absence of value

**Numbers**

* `type()` tells you what type your expression is  
* A need to specify decimals for typing because float stores more in memory  
* x \*\* y \- exponential operator  
* `x // y` \- integer rounded down  
* `x % 4` \- modulo, remainder operator

**Math Functions**

* `round()` \- round up  
* `abs()` \- absolute value

**DEVELOPER FUNDAMENTALS: I**

* Don’t read the dictionary \- You’re never going to memorize everything about a language

**Operator Prescedence**

* Brackets, power of, multiplication/division, addition/subtraction

**Optional: bin() and complex**

* complex data type typically used for complex math  
  * Equivalent to real numbers  
* `bin()` \- returns the binary representation of an integer

**Variables**

* Best practices:  
  * snake\_case  
  * Start with lowercase or underscore  
    1. Starting with underscore typically signifies private variable  
  * Letters, numbers, underscores  
  * Case sensitive  
  * Don’t overwrite keywords  
    1. [Python Keywords](https://www.w3schools.com/python/python_ref_keywords.asp)  
* Constant variables are denoted with capital letters.  
  * They can still be modified despite the best practice.  
* Dunder variables (i.e. `__hi__`) should not be touched.  
* `a,b,c = 1,2,`3 \- shorthand variable assignment

**Expressions vs Statements**

* An expression is a piece of code that produces a value.  
* A statement is an entire line of code that performs an action.

**Augmented Assignment Operator**

* `+=`, `-=`, `*=`

**Strings**

* `‘’’` allows you to create very long strings

**Formatted Strings**

* Python 3 formatted strings with `print(f’STRING {vars}’`)  
* In Python 2 (and 3\)  
  * `print('hi {}. You are {} years old'.format('Johnny', '55'))`  
  * `print('hi {}. You are {} years old'.format(name, age))`  
  * `print('hi {1}. You are {0} years old'.format(name, age))`  
  * `print('hi {new_name}. You are {age} years old'.format(new_name='Sally', age=100))`

**String Indexes**

* `[start:stop:stepover]`  
  * Stepover is how much to iterate by from start to stop  
  * Also known as string slicing  
* Negative indexes start at the end of a string

**Built-In Functions \+ Methods**

* [https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html)  
* [https://www.w3schools.com/python/python\_ref\_string.asp](https://www.w3schools.com/python/python_ref_string.asp)

**DEVELOPER FUNDAMENTALS: II**

* [Commenting Best Practices](https://realpython.com/python-comments-guide/)

**List Slicing**

* `array[start:stop:stepover]` \- same slicing as strings  
* `new_list = old_list[:]` \- Creating a copy of a list.  
* Lists are mutable.

**Matrix**

* Multi-dimensional lists  
* ![][image2]

**List Methods**

* [https://www.w3schools.com/python/python\_ref\_list.asp](https://www.w3schools.com/python/python_ref_list.asp)

**List Methods 3**

* `sorted()` differs from `sort()` such that it creates a copy of the list, then sorts it.

**List Unpacking**

* Assigning variables to values in a list.  
* `a,b,c,*other,... = [array values]`

**Dictionaries**

* A data type in Python, but also a data structure.  
* Storing a group of key/value pairs  
* An unordered key/value pair  
* ![][image3]

**3.7 Update: Ordered Dict**

* As of Python 3.7, dictionaries have been updated to have order maintained.  
* [Python Dictionaries are Ordered now, but how?…and why?](https://medium.com/junior-dev/python-dictionaries-are-ordered-now-but-how-and-why-5d5a40ee327f)

**DEVELOPER FUNDAMENTALS: III**

* When should you use a list and when should you use a dictionary?  
  * Use a list if you need to maintain order.  
    1. Though as of 3.7, dictionaries can maintain order.  
  * Dictionaries are good to hold a lot of different data.

**Dictionary Keys**

* Keys could be a number or boolean, in addition to a string. However, it cannot be something like a list, where the value could be mutable.  
* Key must be unique.

**Dictionary Methods**

* [Dictionary Methods](https://www.w3schools.com/python/python_ref_dictionary.asp)  
* Uncommon way to create dictionary: `dict(key=value)’`  
  * The key has to be variable, cannot be a data type.

**Dictionary Methods 2**

* Can check if key exists in dictionary with `key in dictionary`

**Tuples**

* Tuples are immutable lists  
* Can be used as a valid key in a dictionary  
* ![][image4]

**Tuples 2**

* [Tuple Methods](https://www.w3schools.com/python/python_ref_tuple.asp)

**Sets**

* An unordered collection of unique objects

**Sets 2**

* [Set Methods](https://www.w3schools.com/python/python_ref_set.asp)

**Python Basics 2**  
**Ternary Operator**

* `condition_if_true if condition else condition_if_else`

**is vs \==**

* `==` checks for equality in value  
* `is` checks if the location in memory where the value is stored is the same.  
  * Data structures are always created in a different space in memory, even if the values are the same.

**For Loops**

* `for item in iterable:`

**Iterables**

* An object or a collection that can be iterated over.  
  * List, dictionary, tuple, set, and string are iterables.

**range()**

* Returns a range of numbers based on specified start and finish values  
* `range(start, stop[, step])`  
  * Step is optional, to iterate by how much in the range.

**enumerate()**

* Take an iterable object and return an index for each item in an iterable.  
* Ex. `for i, char in enumerate(‘Helllooo’)`

**While Loops**

* While loops in Python can include an else block for things that don’t satisfy the while condition.   
  * Else block won’t execute if there is a break statement in the loop.

**break, continue, pass**

* `break` will “break” out of the current enclosing loop  
* `continue` says to go back to the top of the enclosing loop, regardless of what happens in the loop until the loop ends  
  * Whatever happens below the `continue` never happens.  
* `pass` does nothing.

**Functions**

* `def` keyword denotes to the Python interpreter that you’re defining a function.

**Docstrings**

* Allows comments in our function so someone will be able to know what the function does.  
* Denoted by `‘’’ Function information ‘’’`  
* `help(<function>)`  
* `<function>.__doc__`

**\*args and \*\*kwargs**

* Using `*args` in function definition allows us to accept any number of positional arguments  
  * Formatted as a tuple  
* Using `**kwargs` in function definition allows us to accept any number of keyword arguments  
  * Formatted as a dictionary  
* Rule: params, \*args, default parameters, \*\*kwargs  
* ![][image5]

**Walrus Operator**

* Denoted as `:=`  
* Assigns values to variables as part of a larger expression.  
* ![][image6]

**Scope Rules**

1. Start with local  
2. Parent local?  
3. Global  
4. Built in Python functions

**global Keyword**

* ![][image7]  
  * Not recommended for use. Use dependency injection in favor of it (passing as an argument to a function).

**nonlocal Keyword**

* Refer to the parent local scope  
* ![][image8]

**Python Exam: Test Your Understanding**

* 21/25 (3/22/2026)

**Advanced Python: Object Oriented Programming**  
**What Is OOP?**

* Objects have methods and attributes that can be accessed using dot (.) notation.

**What Is OOP? Part 2**

* Classes are created in Python by writing `class` ObjectName  
  * Camelcase for writing object names.

**Creating Our Own Objects**

* Dunder method `__init__` is the constructor of a class. Invokes whenever a class object is instantiated.  
* `self` refers to whatever is the left of the dot (`.`). A reference to something that hasn’t been created yet (the class object).  
  * Assigns attributes to the class object when instantiated.  
* ![][image9]

**Attributes and Methods**

* `help(type||object)` will list the blueprint of the class.  
* Class Object Attribute is a static value, defined outside of an instantiated object’s attribute values.  
  * Used for something that does not change.  
  * ![][image10]

**@classmethod and @staticmethod**

* [Instance vs. Static vs. Class Methods in Python: The Important Differences](https://www.makeuseof.com/tag/python-instance-static-class-methods/)  
* `@classmethod` functions have an argument, `cls`, for class  
  * Similar to `self`  
  * `cls` can be used to instantiate a class object in the @classmethod function.  
  * Can be used without instantiating the class.  
  * Not used very often.  
* `@staticmethod` works the same way as `@classmethod`, but does not have access to `cls`.  
  * Used where we don’t care about the class state. Don’t care about the values of the attributes.  
  * Not used very often.

**Encapsulation**

* Binding of data and functions that manipulate data and encapsulate into one object to keep contained in one block.

**Abstraction**

* The hiding of information and giving access to what’s necessary.

**Private vs Public Variables**

* There is no true way to make a variable private in Python.  
* Underscore (`_`) to start a variable name would indicate the variable is private.

**Inheritance**

* New objects take on the properties of existing objects. Inherit classes  
* Pass the parent class name in the brackets of the child class definition.  
  * `class Child(Parent):`

**Inheritance 2**

* `isinstance(instance, Class)` \- checks if something is an instance of a class  
* Every object inherits from the Python base class object

**Polymorphism**

* Object classes can share the same method names, but those method names act differently based on which object calls them.  
* To use the parent class method of the same name in the child class, `parent.method(self)`

**super()**

* To gain access to parent class attributes after initialization, call the `__init__` constructor for the parent class, INSIDE the child class’  `__init__` function.  
  * `Parent.__init__(self, ...attributes)`  
  * `![][image11]`  
* Alternative is to write `super().__init__(attributes)`  
  * `![][image12]`

**Object Introspection**

* The ability to determine the type of an object at runtime.  
* `dir(instance)` gives you a list of methods and attributes of a class.

**Dunder Methods**

* [Dunder Methods](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#special-method-names)

**Exercise: Extending List**

* `issubclass(class, classinfo)`  
  * class \- class to be checked  
  * classinfo \- class to check against  
  * ![][image13]

**Multiple Inheritance**

* Classes can inherit from more than one class just by adding them, separated by commas in the class definition.  
* An issue with multiple inheritance is that attributes aren’t available.  
  * A solution is to have `__init__()` in child class that calls the `__init__()` of the parent classes.  
  * ![][image14]

**MRO \- Method Resolution Order**

* Determines when a method is run, which one is run when there are multiple variants of it  
* `Class.mro()` can be used on a class to show the method resolution order.  
* [MRO Algorithm](https://www.srikanthtechnologies.com/blog/python/mro.aspx)
